summary: The document's unique dominant content region — the main landmark screen readers jump to.
use: Exactly one per page, wrapping everything that is the page's actual subject; give it an id so the skip link can target it.
avoid: Never more than one visible main, and never put site chrome (nav, banners) inside it.

summary: Introductory content for the page or for any article/section — headings, bylines, intro nav.
use: As the page banner (direct child of body, one only) or scoped inside each article/section for its own heading block.
avoid: Don't treat it as a styling wrapper; it must introduce the region it belongs to.

summary: Closing content for the page or any article/section: authorship, related links, legal fine print.
use: As the page contentinfo landmark (direct child of body, one only) or scoped per article for attribution.
avoid: Contact info inside it belongs in an address element, not bare paragraphs.

summary: A major navigation block: primary menu, table of contents, breadcrumbs, pagination.
use: For navigation users will want to jump to; when a page has several, distinguish them with aria-label (Primary, Breadcrumb, Pagination).
avoid: Not every group of links is a nav — a list of footer links or in-prose links usually isn't.

summary: A self-contained composition that would make sense on its own: a post, product card, comment, widget.
use: When the chunk would stand alone in an RSS feed or search result; each gets its own heading, and articles nest (comments inside a post).
avoid: Not for mere page chapters — that's section; not for grouping without standalone meaning — that's div.
snippet:
Overnight Rye Bread

Four ingredients, twelve hours of patience…

kitchen@html-tags.com

summary: A thematic grouping of content with a heading — a chapter of the current page.
use: When content forms a titled chunk of a larger whole; bind it to its heading with aria-labelledby.
avoid: A section without a heading is a smell — use div for pure grouping, or restructure.
attributes: aria-labelledby — points at the section's heading id

summary: Content tangentially related to what surrounds it: pull quotes, related links, sidebars, callouts.
use: When the page would still make complete sense with the block removed.
avoid: Don't use it for content the flow depends on, and label it (aria-labelledby) when it has a heading.
attributes: aria-labelledby — points at the aside's heading
snippet:
Related reading

  • section vs article

summary: Contact information for the nearest article or for the document as a whole.
use: In a page or article footer, wrapping the author's or organization's contact details.
avoid: Not for arbitrary postal addresses in content — it means 'how to contact the responsible party', nothing else.

summary: A landmark wrapping search or filtering functionality — the semantic replacement for role='search'.
use: Around the form (and results controls) for site search, filtering, or any find-things UI.
avoid: It contains the search UI; it is not the input itself — that's input type='search' inside it.
snippet:
<search>
  <form action='/search' method='get'>
    <label for='q'>Search</label>
    <input type='search' id='q' name='q'>
    <button type='submit'>Go</button>
  </form>
</search>

summary: Six levels of section headings that form the document outline screen readers and crawlers navigate by.
use: One h1 per page; pick each level by outline depth, descending one step at a time.
avoid: Never choose a level for its font size, never skip levels going down, and never fake a heading with bold text.
attributes: id — anchor target for deep links

summary: Groups a heading with its subtitle, tagline, or eyebrow paragraphs as one unit.
use: When a heading has a subtitle that belongs to it — title plus tagline, version plus codename.
avoid: Only p elements may accompany the single heading inside; a second heading level does not belong in an hgroup.

The Semantic HTML Vocabulary

A self-demonstrating field guide
